entries.title: *Philosophia Reformata* (1622) — front cover (binding)
entries.creator: Johann Daniel Mylius (engravings: de Bry workshop, Frankfurt)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — Internet Archive copy of the 1622 Frankfurt octavo]
entries.summary:
A bibliographic leaf — the **front cover (binding)** — of the surviving copy of Mylius's *Philosophia Reformata* digitised by the Internet Archive. This is the physical book, not one of its emblems.

## Iconography
Worn calf or sheepskin over boards, blind-tooled and stained by handling. No engraved alchemical scene is present.

## Significance
Recorded honestly as the binding of the volume rather than as an emblem plate. The *Philosophia Reformata* (Frankfurt, 1622) was issued by Lucas Jennis with engravings from the de Bry workshop; this surviving copy carries it through four centuries to the open archive.

## For artists & game designers
A battered leather book cover is a serviceable closed-grimoire asset for a UI or prop.

entries.title: *Philosophia Reformata* (1622) — marbled endpaper (front)
entries.creator: Johann Daniel Mylius (engravings: de Bry workshop, Frankfurt)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — Internet Archive copy of the 1622 Frankfurt octavo]
entries.summary:
A bibliographic leaf — the **marbled endpaper (front)** — of the surviving copy of Mylius's *Philosophia Reformata* digitised by the Internet Archive, not an emblem.

## Iconography
A sheet of hand-combed marbled paper, the swirling 'peacock' or 'nonpareil' pattern in red, ochre and blue that early-modern binders pasted inside the boards. No alchemical imagery is present.

## Significance
Catalogued honestly as binding material rather than as one of the work's emblems. It is nonetheless a witness to the book's life as a physical object — the decorated endpaper framing a treatise on the [[motif:stages of the work|Great Work]]. Decorative marbling spread through German and Dutch workshops in exactly the period of this 1622 Frankfurt printing.

## For artists & game designers
Period marbled paper makes an authentic parchment/inventory-screen texture for an early-modern alchemical setting.

entries.title: *Philosophia Reformata* (1622) — table of contents (*Elenchus*)
entries.creator: Johann Daniel Mylius (engravings: de Bry workshop, Frankfurt)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — Internet Archive copy of the 1622 Frankfurt octavo]
entries.summary:
A letterpress page from the *Elenchus* (table of contents) of Mylius's *Philosophia Reformata* — not an emblem, but the verbal skeleton of the whole programme.

## Iconography
Roman and italic type under running heads such as *Elenchus Libri I* and *De authoritatibus philosophorum chymicorum*, listing the books, *partes* and chapters with leaf references. The stages of the Work appear here by name: *Coniunctio, Conceptio seu Putrefactio, Animae extractio, Ablutio, Sublimatio, Fermentatio, Illuminatio, Fixatio, Multiplicatio, Revivificatio, Perfectionis ostensio*, closing on the *Aenigma Philosophorum sive symbolum Saturni* and the *Colloquium Senioris et Iunioris*.

## Significance
This index is the Rosetta stone for the engraved emblems: it names, in order, the twelve [[motif:stages of the work|degrees of the philosophers]] that the de Bry plates illustrate. Book II, *De authoritatibus philosophorum chymicorum*, is a florilegium of Morienus, Arnaldus of Villanova, Geber, Lull, Maria Prophetissa and other auctores — a map of the alchemical canon Mylius drew upon.

## For artists & game designers
A real period contents page is excellent set-dressing for a discoverable in-world book — and its stage-names are ready-made chapter or quest titles.

entries.title: *Philosophia Reformata* (1622) — text page (Latin letterpress)
entries.creator: Johann Daniel Mylius (engravings: de Bry workshop, Frankfurt)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — Internet Archive copy of the 1622 Frankfurt octavo]
entries.summary:
A page of Latin letterpress prose from the body of Mylius's *Philosophia Reformata*. The Internet Archive scan catalogued under this work is the text octavo; the great majority of its leaves, like this one, are discourse rather than engraved emblems.

## Iconography
Justified Roman type with italic for quotations and printed marginal glosses (e.g. *Putrefactio*, *Solutio philosophica*, *Sol est masculus & Luna foemina*) keying the argument to the named stages of the Work. Running heads read *Philosophiae Reformatae Lib. I* or *De authoritatibus philosophorum chymicorum*. No pictorial emblem is present on the page.

## Significance
These pages carry the doctrine the emblems only gesture at: the generation of metals in the earth, the twelve [[motif:stages of the work|degrees of the philosophers]], the marriage of Sol and Luna, putrefaction and revivification, and (in Book II) a vast florilegium of alchemical *auctores* — Morienus, Arnaldus, Geber, Lull, Maria Prophetissa. Mylius (b. c.1583, Wetter, Hesse) compiled the *Philosophia Reformata* and the *Opus Medico-Chymicum* as systematic syntheses of the Frankfurt Paracelsian-alchemical milieu around Lucas Jennis and the de Bry engravers.

## For artists & game designers
Dense period Latin with marginal stage-labels is ideal background texture for a readable-but-arcane tome; the marginalia double as authentic-looking 'recipe' annotations.

Justified Roman type with italic for quotations and printed marginal glosses (e.g. *Putrefactio*, *Solutio philosophica*, *Sol est masculus & Luna foemina*) keying the argument to the named stages of the Work. Running heads read *Philosophiae Reformatae Lib. I* or *De authoritatibus philosophorum chymicorum*. No pictorial emblem is present on the page.

## Significance
These pages carry the doctrine the emblems only gesture at: the generation of metals in the earth, the twelve [[motif:stages of the work|degrees of the philosophers]], the marriage of Sol and Luna, putrefaction and revivification, and (in Book II) a vast florilegium of alchemical *auctores* — Morienus, Arnaldus, Geber, Lull, Maria Prophetissa. Mylius (b. c.1583, Wetter, Hesse) compiled the *Philosophia Reformata* and the *Opus Medico-Chymicum* as systematic syntheses of the Frankfurt Paracelsian-alchemical milieu around Lucas Jennis and the de Bry engravers.

## For artists & game designers
Dense period Latin with marginal stage-labels is ideal background texture for a readable-but-arcane tome; the marginalia double as authentic-looking 'recipe' annotations.

entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — Internet Archive copy of the 1622 Frankfurt octavo]
entries.summary:
A page of Latin letterpress prose from the body of Mylius's *Philosophia Reformata*. The Internet Archive scan catalogued under this work is the text octavo; the great majority of its leaves, like this one, are discourse rather than engraved emblems.

## Iconography
Justified Roman type with italic for quotations and printed marginal glosses (e.g. *Putrefactio*, *Solutio philosophica*, *Sol est masculus & Luna foemina*) keying the argument to the named stages of the Work. Running heads read *Philosophiae Reformatae Lib. I* or *De authoritatibus philosophorum chymicorum*. No pictorial emblem is present on the page.

## Significance
These pages carry the doctrine the emblems only gesture at: the generation of metals in the earth, the twelve [[motif:stages of the work|degrees of the philosophers]], the marriage of Sol and Luna, putrefaction and revivification, and (in Book II) a vast florilegium of alchemical *auctores* — Morienus, Arnaldus, Geber, Lull, Maria Prophetissa. Mylius (b. c.1583, Wetter, Hesse) compiled the *Philosophia Reformata* and the *Opus Medico-Chymicum* as systematic syntheses of the Frankfurt Paracelsian-alchemical milieu around Lucas Jennis and the de Bry engravers.

## For artists & game designers
Dense period Latin with marginal stage-labels is ideal background texture for a readable-but-arcane tome; the marginalia double as authentic-looking 'recipe' annotations.

citations.text: J. D. Mylius, *Philosophia Reformata* (Frankfurt: Lucas Jennis, 1622).
Stanislas Klossowski de Rola, *The Golden Game: Alchemical Engravings of the Seventeenth Century* (1988).

entries.title: *Philosophia Reformata* — emblem plate: the degrees of the Work (Putrefactio, Conjunctio, Congelatio, Cibatio)
entries.creator: Johann Daniel Mylius (engravings: de Bry workshop, Frankfurt)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — Internet Archive copy of the 1622 Frankfurt octavo]
entries.summary:
One of the genuine engraved emblem plates of Mylius's *Philosophia Reformata* — four numbered alchemical emblems printed together on a single octavo leaf, illustrating consecutive *gradus* (degrees) of the Great Work, with their Latin captions engraved beneath each scene.

## Iconography
Four framed compartments, read like a comic of the laboratory soul. **9. *Putrefactio*** — winged spirits flank a black, rayed sphere, the dead Sun of the *nigredo*, matter rotting so that it may be reborn. **10. *4. Gradus*** — crowned solar and lunar personages sit at a banqueting table while a small figure attends below: the conjunction-feast of King and Queen. **11. *Congelatio*** — a robed figure and a winged genius stand before a furnace and flask, the volatile being fixed. **12. *Cibatio*** — a winged beast or dragon coils within a downward-pointing triangle, the matter being 'fed' (imbibed) with its own moisture. Engraved hills, suns and emblematic borders fill the grounds.

## Significance
These belong to the cycle of twelve [[motif:stages of the work|degrees of the philosophers]] that structures Book I of the *Philosophia Reformata* — separation, conjunction, [[motif:putrefactio|putrefaction]], congelation, cibation, sublimation, fermentation, exaltation, multiplication, projection. Cut in the de Bry / Lucas Jennis workshop at Frankfurt, they distil the same death-and-rebirth grammar as the *Rosarium Philosophorum* into a tight quadripartite plate. The black sun of *Putrefactio* is among the most quoted images of alchemical melancholy.

## For artists & game designers
A ready-made progression UI: four captioned 'stage' cards — death, feast, fixation, feeding — that could serve as the tier icons of a crafting or transmutation skill tree.

entries.title: *Philosophia Reformata* — emblem plate: the degrees of the Work (Sublimatio, Fermentatio, Exaltatio, Multiplicatio)
entries.creator: Johann Daniel Mylius (engravings: de Bry workshop, Frankfurt)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — Internet Archive copy of the 1622 Frankfurt octavo]
entries.summary:
A second engraved emblem leaf from Mylius's *Philosophia Reformata*, continuing the numbered cycle of the degrees of the Work — four captioned scenes printed together above a column of explanatory Latin.

## Iconography
**13. *Sublimatio*** — a crowned king with sceptre presides over a nude figure beside a flask on a pedestal, the subtle spirit rising. **14. *Fermentatio*** — a gaunt figure with a scythe (Saturn / the putrefying earth) stands over a serpent in a furrowed field, the leaven worked into the matter. **15. *Exaltatio*** — an enthroned monarch beneath a draped canopy is flanked by attendants while swine root below, the dignity of the perfected body raised above the base. **16. *Multiplicatio*** — a crowned woman rides a great lion or beast, the tincture multiplying its virtue. Each compartment carries its Latin title and emblematic landscape.

## Significance
With the previous plate this completes the visual programme of the twelve+ degrees that Book I expounds in prose. The scythe-bearing Saturn of *Fermentatio* ties the sequence to the work's Epilogue, the *Aenigma Philosophorum sive symbolum Saturni*; the lion-rider of *Multiplicatio* is the familiar emblem of the increasing power of the [[motif:stages of the work|Stone]]. These cuts, from the Frankfurt de Bry milieu, circulated widely and shaped the later iconography of the *Musaeum Hermeticum*.

## For artists & game designers
Saturn-with-scythe over a serpent, and a queen riding a lion, are strong standalone arcane cards — drop-in art for a 'fermentation' debuff and a 'multiplication' buff respectively.

entries.summary:
A page of Latin letterpress prose from the body of Mylius's *Philosophia Reformata*. The Internet Archive scan catalogued under this work is the text octavo; the great majority of its leaves, like this one, are discourse rather than engraved emblems.

## Iconography
Justified Roman type with italic for quotations and printed marginal glosses (e.g. *Putrefactio*, *Solutio philosophica*, *Sol est masculus & Luna foemina*) keying the argument to the named stages of the Work. Running heads read *Philosophiae Reformatae Lib. I* or *De authoritatibus philosophorum chymicorum*. No pictorial emblem is present on the page.

## Significance
These pages carry the doctrine the emblems only gesture at: the generation of metals in the earth, the twelve [[motif:stages of the work|degrees of the philosophers]], the marriage of Sol and Luna, putrefaction and revivification, and (in Book II) a vast florilegium of alchemical *auctores* — Morienus, Arnaldus, Geber, Lull, Maria Prophetissa. Mylius (b. c.1583, Wetter, Hesse) compiled the *Philosophia Reformata* and the *Opus Medico-Chymicum* as systematic syntheses of the Frankfurt Paracelsian-alchemical milieu around Lucas Jennis and the de Bry engravers.

## For artists & game designers
Dense period Latin with marginal stage-labels is ideal background texture for a readable-but-arcane tome; the marginalia double as authentic-looking 'recipe' annotations.

entries.title: *Philosophia Reformata* (1622) — alphabetical index (*Index*)
entries.creator: Johann Daniel Mylius (engravings: de Bry workshop, Frankfurt)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — Internet Archive copy of the 1622 Frankfurt octavo]
entries.summary:
A letterpress page from the back-of-book alphabetical *Index* of Mylius's *Philosophia Reformata* — a finding-aid rather than an emblem.

## Iconography
Double-column type under the running head *Index*, with catchword entries and leaf references: *Exaltatio virtutis terrae*, *Facies tres operis philosophici*, *Fermentatio*, *Ferrum Alchimicum*, *Filius Lunae*, *Filius ignis* and the like, threading the technical vocabulary of the Work back to its pages.

## Significance
The index reveals how Mylius indexed his own symbolism — *Filius Lunae*, *Aqua coelestis*, *Leo viridis* (the green lion), *Aes Hermetis* — the standing cast of alchemical metaphor. It documents the early-modern apparatus that turned a mystical treatise into a usable reference book on the [[motif:stages of the work|Great Work]].

## For artists & game designers
An alchemical index page is a convincing prop for a searchable in-game codex; its entry terms make flavourful item or spell names (Green Lion, Son of the Moon, Celestial Water).

entries.title: *Philosophia Reformata* (1622) — marbled endpaper (back)
entries.creator: Johann Daniel Mylius (engravings: de Bry workshop, Frankfurt)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — Internet Archive copy of the 1622 Frankfurt octavo]
entries.summary:
A bibliographic leaf — the **marbled endpaper (back)** — of the surviving copy of Mylius's *Philosophia Reformata* digitised by the Internet Archive, not an emblem.

## Iconography
A sheet of hand-combed marbled paper, the swirling 'peacock' or 'nonpareil' pattern in red, ochre and blue that early-modern binders pasted inside the boards. No alchemical imagery is present.

## Significance
Catalogued honestly as binding material rather than as one of the work's emblems. It is nonetheless a witness to the book's life as a physical object — the decorated endpaper framing a treatise on the [[motif:stages of the work|Great Work]]. Decorative marbling spread through German and Dutch workshops in exactly the period of this 1622 Frankfurt printing.

## For artists & game designers
Period marbled paper makes an authentic parchment/inventory-screen texture for an early-modern alchemical setting.

entries.creator: Johann Daniel Mylius (engravings: de Bry workshop, Frankfurt)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — Internet Archive copy of the 1622 Frankfurt octavo]
entries.summary:
A bibliographic leaf — the **back cover (binding)** — of the surviving copy of Mylius's *Philosophia Reformata* digitised by the Internet Archive. This is the physical book, not one of its emblems.

## Iconography
Worn calf or sheepskin over boards, blind-tooled and stained by handling. No engraved alchemical scene is present.

## Significance
Recorded honestly as the binding of the volume rather than as an emblem plate. The *Philosophia Reformata* (Frankfurt, 1622) was issued by Lucas Jennis with engravings from the de Bry workshop; this surviving copy carries it through four centuries to the open archive.

## For artists & game designers
A battered leather book cover is a serviceable closed-grimoire asset for a UI or prop.
